title: Vanilla fudge
subtitle: Condensed milk fudge beaten to a soft-grain finish
excerpt: This is the condensed milk fudge: butter, sugar, and condensed milk cooked to soft-ball stage (116°C), then beaten until it loses its shine and starts to grain. The beating is what makes fudge fudge rather than toffee. Cool, cut into squares. Keeps for two weeks.
sourceNotes: Fudge made from condensed milk, sugar, and butter is documented in British and American domestic confectionery guides from the early twentieth century. The soft-ball stage (115–118°C / 240°F) and the beating technique to induce graining are standard in all domestic fudge references. The condensed milk version (as opposed to the all-milk version) became the British domestic standard from the mid-twentieth century onward. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, documents soft sugar confectionery using similar boiled-sugar and dairy combinations. The specific condensed-milk version is public-domain and reproduced in domestic cooking guides worldwide.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze in a sealed box for up to 3 months. Defrost at room temperature.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Set overnight in the fridge for clean cuts. Returns to room temperature within 20 minutes.

glossaryTerms.definition: Beating is what distinguishes fudge from toffee. Once the cooked mixture cools to about 60°C, vigorous beating with a wooden spoon or a wooden scraper causes the sugar to crystallise into tiny, uniform crystals. The mixture changes from glossy to matt and thickens noticeably; this is the fudge texture forming. Stop beating when the mixture loses its sheen and starts to look slightly rough. Pour it into the tin immediately: fudge that is beaten past this point sets in the pan and cannot be moved.

content.text: Boiled sugar is extremely hot. Stir carefully and keep children out of the kitchen during the cooking stage.

The condensed milk does two things: it adds dairy richness and it stabilises the sugar against over-crystallisation, producing a fine-grained fudge rather than a coarse one. Do not skip the thermometer; the soft-ball stage is the precision point the recipe depends on.

Combine the {{granulated-sugar}}, {{condensed-milk}}, {{unsalted-butter}}, and {{whole-milk}} in a medium, heavy saucepan. Stir over low heat until the sugar dissolves and the butter melts. Increase to medium and clip on the thermometer. Stir frequently to prevent the bottom from scorching. When the temperature reaches 116°C (soft-ball stage), remove from the heat and stir in the {{vanilla-extract}}.
Leave the mixture to cool in the pan to about 60°C (10 to 15 minutes; the pan should feel warm but not hot when you hold the base). Begin
with a wooden spoon. Beat firmly for 5 to 10 minutes until the mixture loses its gloss and starts to thicken and look matt. Pour immediately into a greased and lined 20 cm square tin.

Leave the fudge at room temperature for 30 minutes, then transfer to the fridge to set fully (about 90 minutes). Cut into squares with a sharp knife. Store in a sealed box at room temperature for up to 2 weeks.
